article_title: I gave Claude Code control of my desktop for a week, and it automated things I didn't think were possible
core_thesis: Claude Code, Anthropic's agentic coding assistant, can be used far beyond traditional programming tasks to automate desktop workflows, including controlling applications without APIs, generating images via Python libraries, and remotely managing a PC through its Dispatch feature, demonstrating that AI agents can creatively solve problems using available tools.
detailed_summary: The author, Simon Batt, spent a week experimenting with Claude Code's ability to control his desktop. He started with a simple task: asking Claude to open Calculator, compute 1234×5678, paste the result into Notepad, and save it as result.txt. After granting necessary permissions (Git and computer access), Claude executed the multi-step process successfully. Next, he asked Claude to draw a cat in Paint. Instead of struggling with pixel-level mouse control, Claude autonomously installed the Pillow Python library, wrote a script to mathematically render a cat (circles, polygons, arcs), saved it as a PNG, and opened it in Paint—effectively using Python as a 'smarter brush'. The most impressive feature was Dispatch, which allows sending commands from a phone to a PC. The author used it to remotely launch World of Warcraft: Claude opened Battle.net, booted the game, waited through login, selected a character, and logged in. The article concludes by encouraging readers to experiment creatively with Claude Code beyond coding. The comments section includes criticism that the examples are primitive, but also practical suggestions (e.g., automating email filtering, grocery shopping, CRM building) and warnings about security risks (prompt injection, account bans for LinkedIn automation).

entities.name: Simon Batt
entities.role: Author and experimenter; Computer Science graduate and tech writer.

entities.name: Claude Code
entities.role: Anthropic's agentic coding assistant that can execute terminal commands and control desktop applications.

entities.name: Anthropic
entities.role: AI safety company that developed Claude and Claude Code.

entities.name: Dispatch
entities.role: Claude Code feature allowing remote command sending from a phone to a PC.

entities.name: Pillow
entities.role: Python imaging library used by Claude Code to generate images programmatically.

key_data_points: Claude Code can control desktop applications that lack APIs by simulating human-like interactions (e.g., opening Calculator, Notepad) after granting Git and computer access permissions. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: This capability could be abused for automated reconnaissance or payload delivery if an attacker gains access to a Claude Code instance.)

When asked to draw in Paint, Claude Code autonomously installed the Pillow Python library and wrote a script to mathematically render a cat, saving it as a PNG and opening it in Paint—demonstrating creative problem-solving by using Python as a 'smarter brush'. (🟢 Relevant to programming: Shows how AI can leverage existing libraries to accomplish tasks beyond its direct capabilities.)

The Dispatch feature enables remote control: users can send commands from a phone to a PC, and Claude Code will execute them (e.g., launching World of Warcraft, logging in, selecting a character). (🟢 Relevant to homelab/DevOps: This can be used for remote server management, file uploads, or status checks.)

Commenters reported more advanced uses: one built a CRM and accounting app with Claude Code in a weekend; another automated email filtering and LinkedIn outreach. (🟢 Relevant to ambition: Demonstrates that Claude Code can be a powerful tool for rapid prototyping and automation.)

Security concerns were raised: prompt injection could lead to data theft, and LinkedIn automation risks permanent account bans. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: Highlights attack vectors and the importance of sandboxing AI agents.)
methodology_steps: Install Claude Code and ensure Git is available on the system.
Grant Claude Code permissions to access the computer (e.g., file system, execute commands).
Provide a plain-English prompt describing the desired task (e.g., 'Open Calculator, calculate 1234×5678, paste result into Notepad, save as result.txt').

Claude Code will autonomously execute the steps: open applications, perform actions, and report back. For tasks requiring libraries (e.g., drawing), it may install dependencies (e.g., Pillow) and write scripts.
To use Dispatch: connect your phone to the PC via the Dispatch feature, then send commands remotely. Claude Code will execute them and notify you upon completion or error.
conclusions: Claude Code is capable of far more than just coding; it can automate complex desktop workflows by creatively combining tools (e.g., using Python libraries to generate images). This makes it a versatile assistant for both technical and non-technical tasks.
The Dispatch feature enables remote PC management, which is particularly useful for homelab enthusiasts who need to start servers, check status, or transfer files while away.

Security and privacy risks are significant: granting full desktop access to an AI agent opens vectors for prompt injection and data exfiltration. Users should sandbox Claude Code and avoid exposing sensitive data.

The article's examples are intentionally simple to illustrate capabilities, but commenters show that Claude Code can be used for substantial projects like building custom CRMs or automating business workflows, aligning with the user's ambition to leverage AI for productivity.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your interests in AI tools, automation, and programming. As a programmer, you can use Claude Code to rapidly prototype scripts, automate repetitive tasks (e.g., file sorting, server management), and even build full applications (as commenters did with a CRM). For your homelab/DevOps side, the Dispatch feature offers remote control capabilities that can streamline server administration. From a Red Teaming perspective, understanding Claude Code's capabilities and attack surface (prompt injection, data exfiltration) is valuable for both offensive and defensive security. The article also provides a practical starting point for experimenting with AI agents, which aligns with your ambition to take initiative and explore cutting-edge tools.
extra_advice: Consider setting up a sandboxed environment (e.g., a VM or Docker container) to experiment with Claude Code safely. Try automating a multi-step homelab task, such as provisioning a new server, configuring a firewall rule, or deploying a container—this will give you hands-on experience with agentic AI in a DevOps context. Also, explore the security implications by attempting prompt injection attacks on your own instance to understand how to defend against them.
